request.question: When was the star of You Can Count on Me who also stared in "Ferris Bueller's Day Off" born?

title: Parker Lewis Can't Lose
Matthew Broderick
Alan Ruck
Danke Schoen
You Can Count On Me
Save Ferris
Ferris Bueller's Day Off
Sam Freed
Ferris Bueller (TV series)
Stephanie Blake
sentences: Parker Lewis Can't Lose is an American teen sitcom that originally aired on FOX from September 1990 to June 1993.
During the last season, the series sported the simpler title Parker Lewis.
The series was produced by Columbia Pictures Television and was strongly influenced by the feature film "Ferris Bueller's Day Off".
In competition, NBC debuted the film's TV spinoff "Ferris Bueller", but it only lasted 13 episodes, even though it aired during the same month as "Parker Lewis".
Matthew Broderick (born March 21, 1962) is an American actor and singer.

His roles include the title character in "Ferris Bueller's Day Off" (1986) for which he earned a Golden Globe nomination for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy, the adult voice of Simba in Disney's "The Lion King" trilogy (1994–2004), David Lightman in the Cold War thriller "WarGames" (1983), and Leo Bloom in the Broadway production of "The Producers".
Alan Douglas Ruck (born July 1, 1956) is an American actor.

He played Cameron Frye, Ferris Bueller's hypochondriac best friend in John Hughes' "Ferris Bueller's Day Off" (1986), and Stuart Bondek, the lecherous, power-hungry member of the mayor's staff in the ABC sitcom "Spin City".
His other notable films include "Bad Boys" (1983), "Three Fugitives" (1989), "Young Guns II" (1990), "Speed" (1994), "Twister" (1996), and "Kickin' It Old Skool" (2007).
In 2016, he co-starred with Geena Davis in an updated Fox TV adaptation of William Peter Blatty's best-selling novel "The Exorcist."
"Danke Schoen" is a 1962 song first recorded by Bert Kaempfert.
It gained its fame in 1963 when American singer Wayne Newton recorded his version of it.
It regained fame when it was featured in the 1986 American comedy film "Ferris Bueller's Day Off", lip synced by the main character, Ferris Bueller (Matthew Broderick).
The music was composed by Bert Kaempfert, with the German lyrics written by Kurt Schwabach and the English lyrics by Milt Gabler.
The melody was originally released without lyrics under the title "Candlelight Cafe".
You Can Count On Me is a 2000 American drama film starring Laura Linney, Mark Ruffalo, Rory Culkin, and Matthew Broderick.
Written and directed by Kenneth Lonergan, it tells the story of Sammy, a single mother living in a small town, and her complicated relationships with family and friends.
The story takes place in the fictionalized Catskill communities of Scottsville and Auburn, New York.
The film was primarily shot in and around Margaretville, New York.
Save Ferris is a ska punk band formed circa 1995 in Orange County, California.
Their name is a reference to the 1986 film "Ferris Bueller's Day Off".
Ferris Bueller's Day Off is a 1986 American teen comedy film written, co-produced and directed by John Hughes, and co-produced by Tom Jacobson.
The film stars Matthew Broderick as Ferris Bueller, a high-school slacker who spends a day off from school, with Mia Sara and Alan Ruck.
Ferris regularly "breaks the fourth wall" to explain techniques and inner thoughts.
Sam Freed (born August 29, 1948) is an American actor who has performed on Broadway and television and in movies.
His first major regular role on television was as Bob Barsky in the last three seasons of "Kate & Allie".
In the short-lived series "Ferris Bueller", he played Bill Bueller, the father of the title character.
He also portrayed James C. Whiting III, the executive editor of "The Baltimore Sun", in the fifth and final season of "The Wire".
He is married to actress Barrie Youngfellow, who co-starred in the 1980s ABC-TV/syndicated sitcom series "It's a Living".
Ferris Bueller is an American sitcom based on the 1986 John Hughes film "Ferris Bueller's Day Off".
The show stars Charlie Schlatter in the title role.
The series debuted on August 23, 1990, on NBC and was cancelled within its first season, a few months after its debut.
The show was produced by Paramount Television.
Hughes was not involved in the show's production.
Stephanie Blake is an American burlesque performer and actress.
She is known for playing the role of the nurse who came to Ferris' house in "Ferris Bueller's Day Off".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

- Bridge entity and ambiguity:

  - Missing/ambiguous facts preventing answer: The supplied passages do NOT include the You Can Count on Me page or its cast list, so we cannot determine which Ferris Bueller actor is the one who starred in You Can Count on Me.

- Prioritized next-hop retrievals to resolve ambiguity:
  1) Fetch the "You Can Count on Me" (2000 film) page — extract the infobox "Starring" field and the "Cast" section (keywords: "Starring", "Cast") to identify which actor(s) from Ferris Bueller appear in that film.
  2) If the matching actor is Alan Ruck: no further fetch needed for birth date (birth date available in [7]: "born July 1, 1956"). If the matching actor is Matthew Broderick or Mia Sara (or multiple), fetch the matching actor page(s) — "Matthew Broderick" and/or "Mia Sara" — and extract the birth date from the lead sentence or infobox (keyword: "born" / birth date).
  3) If multiple Ferris Bueller actors appear in You Can Count on Me, fetch each of their pages and extract each birth date (lead sentence/infobox).

- Note: The authoritative "You Can Count on Me" film page is absent from the provided passages and must be retrieved first to identify the correct actor.
response.hop2_query: You Can Count on Me (2000) cast "Starring" list
hop2_docs: You Can Count On Me (disambiguation) | You Can Count On Me is a 2000 American film.

You Can Count On Me | You Can Count On Me is a 2000 American drama film starring Laura Linney, Mark Ruffalo, Rory Culkin, and Matthew Broderick.  Written and directed by Kenneth Lonergan, it tells the story of Sammy, a single mother living in a small town, and her complicated relationships with family and friends.  The story takes place in the fictionalized Catskill communities of Scottsville and Auburn, New York.  The film was primarily shot in and around Margaretville, New York.
You Can't Count on Me | "You Can't Count on Me" is the second single and lead commercial single released by Counting Crows from their album "Saturday Nights & Sunday Mornings".
You Can Count On Me (Panda Bear song) | "You Can Count on Me" is the second single to be released by Panda Bear from "Tomboy".  It was released October 19, 2010 by Domino Records.
You Can Count On Me (Luv Bug song) | You Can Count On Me was the Irish entry in the Eurovision Song Contest 1986, performed in English by Luv Bug.  It was written by Kevin Sheerin.

Kenneth Lonergan | Kenneth Lonergan (born October 16, 1962) is an American film director, playwright, and screenwriter.  He is best known for co-writing "Gangs of New York" (2002), and for writing and directing "You Can Count On Me" (2000), "Margaret" (2011), and "Manchester by the Sea" (2016).  Lonergan earned an Academy Award for Best Director nomination for "Manchester by the Sea", and was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay for "You Can Count On Me", "Gangs of New York", and "Manchester by the Sea", winning for the latter at the 89th Academy Awards.  He also won the BAFTA Award for Best Original Screenplay for "Manchester by the Sea" at the 70th British Academy Film Awards.

You Can Depend on Me (song) | "You Can Depend on Me" is a song written by Charles Carpenter, Louis Dunlap and Earl "Fatha" Hines and performed by several people, including Louis Armstrong (1931 and 1951), Count Basie (1939), Earl Hines himself (1940), Lester Young (1956), Nat King Cole (1957) and Brenda Lee (1961).  Lee's "You Can Depend on Me" reached No.6 on the Billboard Pop Singles chart in April 1961.  In 1962 it was featured on Lee's album "Brenda, That's All".
